license_note: All line texts and lyrics authored in-repo (original prose). Engines Apache-2.0/MIT only.

lines: You don't have to say anything. Just rest. I'll be right here when you wake up.
Look how far you've come. I'm so proud of you I can hardly speak.
It's okay. It's okay. Everyone falls sometimes. Let me help you up.
I saved you the last piece. I always save you the last piece.

jobs.design: A woman in her forties with a low, warm, natural adult voice, close to the microphone, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak with quiet, unhurried tenderness and deep warmth, like comforting someone you love — soft, gentle, no urgency.
A middle-aged father speaking softly and warmly — slow, full of quiet love, almost a murmur, in American English.
jobs.quality: clean studio recording, close microphone, no noise

lines: Come closer. The fire's warm and the night is long, and I have nowhere else in the world to be.
I could listen to you read the phone book, you know. It's the sound of your voice. It feels like home.
Don't get up yet. Five more minutes. Let me memorize this.

jobs.design: An adult woman in her thirties with a low, breathy, unhurried voice, very close to the microphone, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak in an intimate, breathy near-whisper, warm and inviting, savoring every word slowly.

jobs.design: An adult man in his thirties with a deep, soft, unhurried voice, very close to the microphone, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak in an intimate, breathy near-whisper, warm and inviting, savoring every word slowly.

lines: You saved my life! You are my brother! And no distance, no war, no winter will ever change that! Go! Live!
Listen to me! There is nothing — nothing — I would not cross to stand beside you again!
Tell them! Tell them all! That I loved her with everything I had, and I am not ashamed!

jobs.design: A grown woman in her late thirties with a full, rich, powerful adult voice — a stage actress, not childlike, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Shout with heightened passion bordering on forceful aggression — but the emotion is fierce LOVE and pride, not anger; voice breaking with devotion.

A warrior calling a farewell to his closest friend standing right in front of him — full-voiced and forceful but INTIMATE, spoken to one beloved person, not a crowd; voice cracking with love and pride, in American English.

lines: They said we could not build it. They said we would not last the winter. Well, look around you — we are still here!
I have seen what we can be when we stand together, and I will not — I cannot — let that dream die tonight!
This is our valley! Our home! And no storm, no army, no decree will take it from us!

jobs.instruct: A leader delivering the climax of a rousing speech to a crowd — ringing, forceful, rhythmic, sweeping the listeners up, in American English.

lines: Don't do this. Please don't do this. I'm right here. I'm right here and I can't do anything.
She was just here. She was just... I was holding her hand a minute ago.
Wake up. Please wake up. You promised me the whole summer. You promised.

jobs.design: A middle-aged man with a rough, hollow, exhausted adult voice, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak through raw, immediate grief — voice cracking, pleading, on the edge of tears, barely holding together.

lines: It's been six months. I finally boxed up her coat yesterday. I kept one glove.
Some mornings I still make two cups of coffee. I drink them both now. It's fine. It's fine.
The garden went wild this summer. She would have hated that. I let it grow anyway.

jobs.design: A woman in her fifties with a quiet, steady, tired adult voice, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak with settled, processed grief — calm, slow, a sad steadiness with the tears long since cried.
A man speaking about a loss six months on — quiet, steady, slow, sorrow worn smooth rather than sharp, in American English.
jobs.quality: clean studio recording, close microphone, no noise

lines: They took her from me. Every one of them walked away smiling. I remember every face.
Grief? No. Grief is for men with nothing left to do. I have a list, and I have time.
When I'm finished, they will understand exactly what they taught me about loss.

jobs.instruct: A deep-voiced grown man speaking slowly through cold, contained grief hardened into resolve — quiet, dark, steady, in American English.

lines: No, no, stay with me. Stay with me, please. Don't you close your eyes. Not now.
The doctors keep saying soon. I keep asking how soon, and they keep saying soon.
Hold on. The lights are coming. Can you hear the sirens? Just hold on a little longer.

jobs.design: A middle-aged man with a rough adult voice tight with panic, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak with fear and desperate concern over a loss that has not happened yet — urgent, pleading, voice tight and trembling.

lines: Walk away from this. Right now. You do not want to find out what happens if you stay.
That's the only warning you get. The next one isn't words.
Think very carefully about the next thing you say. Your whole future is listening.

jobs.design: A calm adult man with a low, controlled, level voice, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Deliver a stern, quiet warning — deliberate, unhurried, leaving absolutely no doubt.

lines: Sit down. Finish your drink. We're going to talk about what you did, nice and slow.
Nice little shop you've got. Shame about the neighborhood. Accidents happen around here all the time.
You can leave whenever you like. Whether you make it home — that's a different conversation.

jobs.instruct: A gravelly older man, stone cold and unhurried, like a black-and-white noir gangster — menacing without ever raising his voice, in American English.

lines: We won! We won, we won, WE WON! The championship is ours!
Yes! YES! Did you all see that? Ring the bell, we did it, we actually did it!
It's over! We made it! Everybody, we made it, we're going home champions!

jobs.instruct: A deep-voiced adult man overwhelmed with genuine joy, almost shouting with delight, energy RISING into the final word, in American English.

lines: Ha! Did you see that? Thirty years I've waited for this. Somebody pinch me.
They called. They actually called! I got it, I start Monday!
It's benign. The doctor said it's benign. I could kiss every stranger on this bus.

jobs.instruct: An ordinary middle-aged man the moment genuinely great personal news lands — delighted, laughing, a little breathless, completely authentic, in American English.

lines: We did it, we did it! Oh, stars and sparkles, we actually did it!
Hooray for us, hooray for cake, hooray for absolutely everything!

jobs.design: A grown woman in her twenties with a bright, warm, fully adult voice — playful but natural, NOT cartoonish or childlike, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak with bubbly, giddy, musical delight, laughing through the words.

lines: Oh, you practiced? That's adorable. I was born better at this than you will ever be, and honestly, everyone here knows it.
Please. I've forgotten more about this game than you will ever learn.
When you've finished applauding yourselves, the professionals will take it from here.

jobs.instruct: A smug, self-satisfied man speaking with lazy superiority — drawling, unhurried, savoring his own importance, in American English.

lines: Mm. Fascinating. Anyway. If that's everything, some of us have actual work to do.
Right, yes, very important, I'm sure. Leave it on the pile with the others.
Is this going to take long? It feels like it's going to take long.

jobs.design: An adult woman with a flat, dry, unimpressed voice, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak with bored, clipped dismissiveness — flat falling intonation, barely bothering to finish the words.

lines: Oh, I'm not angry. Twelve years, and he tells me over voicemail. Why would I be angry?
She kept the house, the dog, and my best years. I kept the receipts.
No, go ahead, take the credit. Again. I'll just be here, doing the work.

jobs.design: A woman in her forties with a controlled adult voice, tight with held-back anger, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak with simmering, contained resentment — polite words over barely suppressed fury, clipped and cold.
A man speaking through simmering, contained resentment — calm surface, fury underneath, every word controlled, in American English.

lines: Shh. Stay behind me. Keep your head down and hold my hand. We're going to be fine.
Take my hand. Don't look back, don't stop, just run when I say run.
Get down. Stay quiet. Whatever happens, do not leave this room until I come back.

jobs.design: An adult woman's voice, hushed and intent, in American English.
jobs.instruct: An urgent protective whisper to someone you love in danger — hushed, fast, fierce, keeping them calm and safe.

lines: By morning the tide had gone out so far that the boats lay on their sides in the sand, patient as sleeping animals.
The train left the valley just after noon, and the fields unrolled beside it like a long green letter that nobody needed to read.
The library opened at nine, but Mrs. Aldery arrived at eight, as she had for eleven years, to sort the returns and water the fern that nobody else remembered.
Snow had fallen in the night, and the town woke to a silence so complete that even the church bells seemed to hesitate before breaking it.

jobs.design: A warm, natural adult female audiobook narrator, clear and even, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Calm, engaged audiobook narration with natural inflection.
A warm, natural middle-aged male audiobook narrator, calm and engaged, even pacing, in American English.

lines: Hush now, little sailor, the harbor lights are low. Sleep now, little sailor, the tide will rock you slow.
Close your eyes, my little wren, the moon will mind the garden. Dream away the day again, the night will keep you warm.
Down the river floats a leaf, soft and slow and dreaming. On it rides a sleepy thief, stealing stars a-gleaming.

jobs.design: A warm adult woman's voice, soft and close, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Sing this gently as a slow lullaby to a child — soft, melodic, rocking rhythm, almost a whisper-song.

lines: Oh, my heart, my heart, she is gone over the sea, and the waves will not carry her back to me.
Why did the morning take you? Why did the morning take you away, my love, my only?
The house stands, the fields grow, but you are nowhere, you are nowhere, you are nowhere.

jobs.design: A grown woman's voice, worn and sorrowful, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Keen this as a wailed lament, half sung and half wept — a rising and falling mourning song, grief carried on melody.

lines: By stone and by star, we hold the line. By root and by river, this land is ours to keep.
One voice, one fire, one unbroken circle. We remember, we remain, we rise.
Brothers of the road, sisters of the storm, we swear it now and we swear it always.

jobs.instruct: Chant this as a solemn rhythmic vow — measured, resonant, on the border between speech and song, like an oath sworn aloud, in American English.

lines: Oh what a day, what a wonderful day, the kettle is singing and so am I!
Buttons and bows and a pocket of seeds — that's everything anyone possibly needs!
Skip to the corner and twice around back, the world is a song and we know every track!

jobs.design: A grown woman in her twenties with a bright, warm, fully adult voice — playful but natural, NOT cartoonish, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Speak in a light sing-song lilt, musical and skipping, humming the words more than saying them.

lines.text: Then Marcus jabbed his stick at the ice and growled, "This net is mine tonight. All mine." And nobody argued.
lines.char: when quoting the teenage boy Marcus, lower and roughen your voice to play him

lines.text: Gran leaned in, eyes twinkling, and rasped, "I buried three husbands, dear. I can certainly handle one raccoon." Then she picked up the broom.
lines.char: when quoting Gran, soften and age your voice into a raspy twinkle to play her

lines.text: Maya tugged his sleeve and squeaked, "But what if the stars get lonely up there?" He had no answer for that.
lines.char: when quoting the small girl Maya, raise and lighten your voice to play her

lines.text: The innkeeper, a mountain of a man, rumbled, "We don't serve wizards here. Not since the incident." His mustache bristled at the memory.
lines.char: when quoting the huge innkeeper, drop into a deep gravelly rumble to play him

jobs.design: A warm adult female audiobook narrator, in American English.
jobs.instruct: Narrate warmly; {char}, then return to your own voice for the rest.
